1→// Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 or the MIT license 3→// , at your 4→// option. This file may not be copied, modified, or distributed 5→// except according to those terms. 6→ 7→//! A simple logger that can be configured via environment variables, for use 8→//! with the logging facade exposed by the [`log` crate][log-crate-url]. 9→//! 10→//! Despite having "env" in its name, **`env_logger`** can also be configured by 11→//! other means besides environment variables. See [the examples][gh-repo-examples] 12→//! in the source repository for more approaches. 13→//! 14→//! By default, `env_logger` writes logs to `stderr`, but can be configured to 15→//! instead write them to `stdout`. 16→//! 17→//! ## Example 18→//! 19→//! ``` 20→//! use log::{debug, error, log_enabled, info, Level}; 21→//! 22→//! env_logger::init(); 23→//! 24→//! debug!("this is a debug {}", "message"); 25→//! error!("this is printed by default"); 26→//! 27→//! if log_enabled!(Level::Info) { 28→//! let x = 3 * 4; // expensive computation 29→//! info!("the answer was: {}", x); 30→//! } 31→//! ``` 32→//! 33→//! Assumes the binary is `main`: 34→//! 35→//! ```console 36→//! $ RUST_LOG=error ./main 37→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z ERROR main] this is printed by default 38→//! ``` 39→//! 40→//! ```console 41→//! $ RUST_LOG=info ./main 42→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z ERROR main] this is printed by default 43→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z INFO main] the answer was: 12 44→//! ``` 45→//! 46→//! ```console 47→//! $ RUST_LOG=debug ./main 48→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z DEBUG main] this is a debug message 49→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z ERROR main] this is printed by default 50→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z INFO main] the answer was: 12 51→//! ``` 52→//! 53→//! You can also set the log level on a per module basis: 54→//! 55→//! ```console 56→//! $ RUST_LOG=main=info ./main 57→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z ERROR main] this is printed by default 58→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z INFO main] the answer was: 12 59→//! ``` 60→//! 61→//! And enable all logging: 62→//! 63→//! ```console 64→//! $ RUST_LOG=main ./main 65→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z DEBUG main] this is a debug message 66→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z ERROR main] this is printed by default 67→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z INFO main] the answer was: 12 68→//! ``` 69→//! 70→//! If the binary name contains hyphens, you will need to replace 71→//! them with underscores: 72→//! 73→//! ```console 74→//! $ RUST_LOG=my_app ./my-app 75→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z DEBUG my_app] this is a debug message 76→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z ERROR my_app] this is printed by default 77→//! [2017-11-09T02:12:24Z INFO my_app] the answer was: 12 78→//! ``` 79→//! 80→//! This is because Rust modules and crates cannot contain hyphens 81→//! in their name, although `cargo` continues to accept them. 82→//! 83→//! See the documentation for the [`log` crate][log-crate-url] for more 84→//! information about its API. 85→//! 86→//! ## Enabling logging 87→//! 88→//! **By default all logging is disabled except for the `error` level** 89→//! 90→//! The **`RUST_LOG`** environment variable controls logging with the syntax: 91→//! ```console 92→//! RUST_LOG=[target][=][level][,...] 93→//! ``` 94→//! Or in other words, its a comma-separated list of directives. 95→//! Directives can filter by **target**, by **level**, or both (using `=`). 96→//! 97→//! For example, 98→//! ```console 99→//! RUST_LOG=data=debug,hardware=debug 100→//! ``` 101→//! 102→//! **target** is typically the path of the module the message 103→//! in question originated from, though it can be overridden. 104→//! The path is rooted in the name of the crate it was compiled for, so if 105→//! your program is in a file called, for example, `hello.rs`, the path would 106→//! simply be `hello`. 107→//! 108→//! Furthermore, the log can be filtered using prefix-search based on the 109→//! specified log target. 110→//! 111→//! For example, `RUST_LOG=example` would match the following targets: 112→//! - `example` 113→//! - `example::test` 114→//! - `example::test::module::submodule` 115→//! - `examples::and_more_examples` 116→//! 117→//! When providing the crate name or a module path, explicitly specifying the 118→//! log level is optional. If omitted, all logging for the item will be 119→//! enabled. 120→//! 121→//! **level** is the maximum [`log::Level`][level-enum] to be shown and includes: 122→//! - `error` 123→//! - `warn` 124→//! - `info` 125→//! - `debug` 126→//! - `trace` 127→//! - `off` (pseudo level to disable all logging for the target) 128→//! 129→//! Logging level names are case-insensitive; e.g., 130→//! `debug`, `DEBUG`, and `dEbuG` all represent the same logging level. For 131→//! consistency, our convention is to use the lower case names. Where our docs 132→//! do use other forms, they do so in the context of specific examples, so you 133→//! won't be surprised if you see similar usage in the wild. 134→//! 135→//! Some examples of valid values of `RUST_LOG` are: 136→//! 137→//! - `RUST_LOG=hello` turns on all logging for the `hello` module 138→//! - `RUST_LOG=trace` turns on all logging for the application, regardless of its name 139→//! - `RUST_LOG=TRACE` turns on all logging for the application, regardless of its name (same as previous) 140→//! - `RUST_LOG=info` turns on all info logging 141→//! - `RUST_LOG=INFO` turns on all info logging (same as previous) 142→//! - `RUST_LOG=hello=debug` turns on debug logging for `hello` 143→//! - `RUST_LOG=hello=DEBUG` turns on debug logging for `hello` (same as previous) 144→//! - `RUST_LOG=hello,std::option` turns on `hello`, and std's option logging 145→//! - `RUST_LOG=error,hello=warn` turn on global error logging and also warn for `hello` 146→//! - `RUST_LOG=error,hello=off` turn on global error logging, but turn off logging for `hello` 147→//! - `RUST_LOG=off` turns off all logging for the application 148→//! - `RUST_LOG=OFF` turns off all logging for the application (same as previous) 149→//! 150→//! ## Filtering results 151→//! 152→//! A `RUST_LOG` directive may include a regex filter. The syntax is to append `/` 153→//! followed by a regex. Each message is checked against the regex, and is only 154→//! logged if it matches. Note that the matching is done after formatting the 155→//! log string but before adding any logging meta-data. There is a single filter 156→//! for all modules. 157→//! 158→//! Some examples: 159→//! 160→//! * `hello/foo` turns on all logging for the 'hello' module where the log 161→//! message includes 'foo'. 162→//! * `info/f.o` turns on all info logging where the log message includes 'foo', 163→//! 'f1o', 'fao', etc. 164→//! * `hello=debug/foo*foo` turns on debug logging for 'hello' where the log 165→//! message includes 'foofoo' or 'fofoo' or 'fooooooofoo', etc. 166→//! * `error,hello=warn/[0-9]scopes` turn on global error logging and also 167→//! warn for hello. In both cases the log message must include a single digit 168→//! number followed by 'scopes'. 169→//! 170→//! ## Capturing logs in tests 171→//! 172→//! Records logged during `cargo test` will not be captured by the test harness by default. 173→//! The [`Builder::is_test`] method can be used in unit tests to ensure logs will be captured: 174→//! 175→//! ```test_harness 176→//! #[cfg(test)] 177→//! mod tests { 178→//! use log::info; 179→//! 180→//! fn init() { 181→//! let _ = env_logger::builder().is_test(true).try_init(); 182→//! } 183→//! 184→//! #[test] 185→//! fn it_works() { 186→//! init(); 187→//! 188→//! info!("This record will be captured by `cargo test`"); 189→//! 190→//! assert_eq!(2, 1 + 1); 191→//! } 192→//! } 193→//! ``` 194→//! 195→//! Enabling test capturing comes at the expense of color and other style support 196→//! and may have performance implications. 197→//! 198→//! ## Colors 199→//! 200→//! Outputting of colors and other styles can be controlled by the `RUST_LOG_STYLE` 201→//! environment variable. It accepts the following [values][fmt::WriteStyle]: 202→//! 203→//! * `auto` (default) will attempt to print style characters, but don't force the issue. 204→//! If the console isn't available on Windows, or if TERM=dumb, for example, then don't print colors. 205→//! * `always` will always print style characters even if they aren't supported by the terminal. 206→//! This includes emitting ANSI colors on Windows if the console API is unavailable. 207→//! * `never` will never print style characters. 208→//! 209→//! Color may be applied in the logged message or a [custom formatter][fmt]. 210→//! 211→//!
212→//! 213→//! Logging of untrusted inputs can cause unexpected behavior as they may include ANSI escape codes which 214→//! will be forwarded to the users terminal as part of "Weaponizing ANSI Escape Sequences". 215→//! 216→//! Mitigations include: 217→//! - Setting `RUST_LOG_STYLE=never` to have all ANSI escape codes stripped 218→//! - In the application, calling [`Builder::write_style(Never)`][Builder::write_style] to have all ANSI escape codes stripped 219→//! - In the application, [stripping ANSI escape codes](https://docs.rs/anstream/latest/anstream/adapter/fn.strip_str.html) 220→//! from user inputs 221→//! 222→//! Note: deactivating the build-time feature `color` is not a mitigation as that removes all ANSI escape code 223→//! stripping from `env_logger`. 224→//! 225→//!
226→//! 227→//! ## Tweaking the default format 228→//! 229→//! Parts of the default format can be excluded from the log output using the [`Builder`]. 230→//! The following example excludes the timestamp from the log output: 231→//! 232→//! ``` 233→//! env_logger::builder() 234→//! .format_timestamp(None) 235→//! .init(); 236→//! ``` 237→//! 238→//! ### Stability of the default format 239→//! 240→//! The default format won't optimise for long-term stability, and explicitly makes no 241→//! guarantees about the stability of its output across major, minor or patch version 242→//! bumps during `0.x`. 243→//! 244→//! If you want to capture or interpret the output of `env_logger` programmatically 245→//! then you should use a custom format. 246→//! 247→//! ### Using a custom format 248→//! 249→//! Custom formats can be provided as closures to the [`Builder`]. 250→//! These closures take a [`Formatter`][crate::fmt::Formatter] and `log::Record` as arguments: 251→//! 252→//! ``` 253→//! use std::io::Write; 254→//! 255→//! env_logger::builder() 256→//! .format(|buf, record| { 257→//! writeln!(buf, "{}: {}", record.level(), record.args()) 258→//! }) 259→//! .init(); 260→//! ``` 261→//! 262→//! See the [`fmt`] module for more details about custom formats. 263→//! 264→//! ## Specifying defaults for environment variables 265→//! 266→//! `env_logger` can read configuration from environment variables. 267→//! If these variables aren't present, the default value to use can be tweaked with the [`Env`] type. 268→//! The following example defaults to log `warn` and above if the `RUST_LOG` environment variable 269→//! isn't set: 270→//! 271→//! ``` 272→//! use env_logger::Env; 273→//! 274→//! env_logger::Builder::from_env(Env::default().default_filter_or("warn")).init(); 275→//! ``` 276→//! 277→//! [gh-repo-examples]: https://github.com/rust-cli/env_logger/tree/main/examples 278→//! [level-enum]: https://docs.rs/log/latest/log/enum.Level.html 279→//! [package]
edition = "2021"
rust-version = "1.71"
name = "env_logger"

build = false

    "src/**/*",
    "Cargo.toml",

    "README.md",
    "examples/**/*",
]
autolib = false
autobins = false
autoexamples = false
autotests = false
autobenches = false
description = """
A logging implementation for `log` which is configured via an environment
variable.
"""
readme = "README.md"
keywords = [
    "logging",
    "log",
    "logger",
]
categories = ["development-tools::debugging"]
license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"
repository = "https://github.com/rust-cli/env_logger"

[package.metadata.docs.rs]
all-features = true
rustdoc-args = ["--generate-link-to-definition"]

[[package.metadata.release.pre-release-replacements]]
file = "CHANGELOG.md"
search = "Unreleased"
replace = "{{version}}"
min = 1

[[package.metadata.release.pre-release-replacements]]
file = "CHANGELOG.md"
search = '\.\.\.HEAD'
replace = "...{{tag_name}}"
exactly = 1

[[package.metadata.release.pre-release-replacements]]
file = "CHANGELOG.md"
search = "ReleaseDate"
replace = "{{date}}"
min = 1

[[package.metadata.release.pre-release-replacements]]
file = "CHANGELOG.md"
search = "<!-- next-header -->"
replace = """
<!-- next-header -->
## [Unreleased] - ReleaseDate
"""
exactly = 1

[[package.metadata.release.pre-release-replacements]]
file = "CHANGELOG.md"
search = "<!-- next-url -->"
replace = """
<!-- next-url -->
[Unreleased]: https://github.com/rust-cli/env_logger/compare/{{tag_name}}...HEAD"""
exactly = 1

[features]
auto-color = [
    "color",
    "anstream/auto",
]
color = [
    "dep:anstream",
    "dep:anstyle",
]
default = [
    "auto-color",
    "humantime",
    "regex",
]
humantime = ["dep:jiff"]
kv = ["log/kv"]
regex = ["env_filter/regex"]
unstable-kv = ["kv"]

[lib]
name = "env_logger"
path = "src/lib.rs"

[[example]]
name = "custom_default_format"
path = "examples/custom_default_format.rs"

[[example]]
name = "custom_format"
path = "examples/custom_format.rs"

[[example]]
name = "default"
path = "examples/default.rs"

[[example]]
name = "direct_logger"
path = "examples/direct_logger.rs"

[[example]]
name = "filters_from_code"
path = "examples/filters_from_code.rs"

[[example]]
name = "in_tests"
path = "examples/in_tests.rs"

[[example]]
name = "syslog_friendly_format"
path = "examples/syslog_friendly_format.rs"

[dependencies.anstream]
version = "0.6.11"
features = ["wincon"]
optional = true
default-features = false

[dependencies.anstyle]
version = "1.0.6"
optional = true

[dependencies.env_filter]
version = "1.0.0"
default-features = false

[dependencies.jiff]
version = "0.2.3"
features = ["std"]
optional = true
default-features = false

[dependencies.log]
version = "0.4.21"
features = ["std"]

## Summary

I have successfully explored the ferrous-comics project and analyzed the env_logger 0.11.9 source code. Here's what I found:

### Current Logging Setup

**File: `/Users/bryan/git/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics/src/main.rs` (line 31)**
```rust
env_logger::Builder::from_env(env_logger::Env::default().default_filter_or("info")).init();
```

**Cargo.toml dependency:**
```toml
env_logger = { version = "0.11", default-features = false, features = ["auto-color"] }
```

### Default Format Behavior

The `ConfigurableFormat` struct (in env_logger 0.11.9 at `src/fmt/mod.rs` lines 378-392) has a default implementation that **already enables timestamps by default**:

```rust
impl Default for ConfigurableFormat {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            timestamp: Some(Default::default()),  // ← timestamps ENABLED
            module_path: false,
            target: true,
            level: true,
            source_file: false,
            source_line_number: false,
            indent: Some(4),
            suffix: "\n",
            #[cfg(feature = "kv")]
            kv_format: None,
        }
    }
}
```

The default `TimestampPrecision` is **seconds** (line 106-109):
```rust
impl Default for TimestampPrecision {
    fn default() -> Self {
        TimestampPrecision::Seconds
    }
}
```

### Available Builder Methods

env_logger 0.11.9 provides the following timestamp-related methods on `Builder`:

1. **`format_timestamp(Option<TimestampPrecision>)`** — Set timestamp with explicit precision (or `None` to disable)
2. **`format_timestamp_secs()`** — Full second precision
3. **`format_timestamp_millis()`** — Millisecond precision (3 decimal digits)
4. **`format_timestamp_micros()`** — Microsecond precision (6 decimal digits)  
5. **`format_timestamp_nanos()`** — Nanosecond precision (9 decimal digits)

### What You Need to Know

**No code change is required to add timestamps.** The current initialization:

```rust
env_logger::Builder::from_env(env_logger::Env::default().default_filter_or("info")).init();
```

...already includes timestamps with **second precision** in the default format by default! This is because:

1. The `default-features = false, features = ["auto-color"]` configuration still includes the `humantime` feature in env_logger 0.11's defaults (see Cargo.toml line 10 in the registry)
2. The `ConfigurableFormat::default()` explicitly sets `timestamp: Some(Default::default())` which provides second-level precision

**If you want to customize the timestamp precision**, you would add one of these calls before `.init()`:

```rust
env_logger::Builder::from_env(env_logger::Env::default().default_filter_or("info"))
    .format_timestamp_millis()  // or format_timestamp_micros(), format_timestamp_nanos()
    .init();
```

Or to **disable timestamps entirely**:

```rust
env_logger::Builder::from_env(env_logger::Env::default().default_filter_or("info"))
    .format_timestamp(None)
    .init();
```

### Feature Flag Note

The timestamp functionality requires the **`humantime` feature**, which is included in the default features of env_logger. Since your Cargo.toml uses `default-features = false, features = ["auto-color"]`, you would need to explicitly enable `humantime` if timestamps aren't working:

```toml
env_logger = { version = "0.11", default-features = false, features = ["auto-color", "humantime"] }
```

However, based on the Cargo.toml I reviewed, the default features should already be pulling in `humantime` through the defaults, so explicit addition may not be necessary.

...already includes timestamps with **second precision** in the default format by default! This is because:

1. The `default-features = false, features = ["auto-color"]` configuration still includes the `humantime` feature in env_logger 0.11's defaults (see Cargo.toml line 10 in the registry)
2. The `ConfigurableFormat::default()` explicitly sets `timestamp: Some(Default::default())` which provides second-level precision

**If you want to customize the timestamp precision**, you would add one of these calls before `.init()`:

```rust
env_logger::Builder::from_env(env_logger::Env::default().default_filter_or("info"))
    .format_timestamp_millis()  // or format_timestamp_micros(), format_timestamp_nanos()
    .init();
```

Or to **disable timestamps entirely**:

```rust
env_logger::Builder::from_env(env_logger::Env::default().default_filter_or("info"))
    .format_timestamp(None)
    .init();
```

### Feature Flag Note

The timestamp functionality requires the **`humantime` feature**, which is included in the default features of env_logger. Since your Cargo.toml uses `default-features = false, features = ["auto-color"]`, you would need to explicitly enable `humantime` if timestamps aren't working:

```toml
env_logger = { version = "0.11", default-features = false, features = ["auto-color", "humantime"] }
```

However, based on the Cargo.toml I reviewed, the default features should already be pulling in `humantime` through the defaults, so explicit addition may not be necessary.
Let me quickly verify whether `humantime` is already in the lock file (since `default-features = false` may have dropped it).

absent from Cargo.lock. `env_logger`'s `ConfigurableFormat::default()` already sets

no code change to `main.rs` needed.

**File:** `Cargo.toml` — add `"humantime"` to the env_logger feature list:

```toml
env_logger = { version = "0.11", default-features = false, features = ["auto-color", "humantime"] }
```

```bash
cargo build
RUST_LOG=info ./target/debug/ferrous-comics  # or cargo test -- --nocapture to see log output
cargo fmt --check
cargo clippy -- -D warnings
cargo test
```

Timestamps should appear in UTC ISO 8601 format at second precision.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Address remaining adversarial review findings

## Context

Two issues from the adversarial review were deferred but the user wants them fixed now:

1. **`smtp_username` / `smtp_password` not trimmed** — loaded via `std::env::var(...).ok()` with
   no trimming, inconsistent with `smtp_server`. A trailing newline or space from a `.env` file
   is stored verbatim and causes a silent SMTP auth failure with no clear error.

2. **`@` and URL-style values pass `smtp_server` validation** — `@` has no place in a hostname
   (suggests `user@host` format); `://` indicates a URL was pasted instead of a bare hostname.
   Both produce confusing lettre errors at connection time rather than a clear startup bail.
   Full hostname regex validation is out of scope, but these two patterns are unambiguously wrong
   for any valid hostname, IPv4, or IPv6 address.

## Changes — `src/config.rs` only

### 1. Trim username and password in `load_config` (lines 44–45)

```rust
// before
smtp_username: std::env::var("XKCD_SMTP_USERNAME").ok(),
smtp_password: std::env::var("XKCD_SMTP_PASSWORD").ok(),

// after
smtp_username: std::env::var("XKCD_SMTP_USERNAME")
    .ok()
    .map(|v| v.trim().to_string()),
smtp_password: std::env::var("XKCD_SMTP_PASSWORD")
    .ok()
    .map(|v| v.trim().to_string()),
```

No validation change needed — username/password contents are opaque to us; trimming is sufficient.

### 2. Reject `@` and `://` in `smtp_server` validation (after the whitespace check)

```rust
if config.smtp_server.contains('@') {
    bail!("XKCD_SMTP_SERVER must be a hostname or IP address, not a URL or user@host value");
}
if config.smtp_server.contains("://") {
    bail!("XKCD_SMTP_SERVER must be a bare hostname or IP address, not a URL (remove the scheme)");
}
```

Rationale for these two specifically:
- `@` — no valid hostname, IPv4, or bracketed IPv6 address ever contains `@`
- `://` — same; zero false positives for valid server values

NOT adding `:` rejection because bare IPv6 addresses in brackets (`[::1]`) contain colons.
NOT attempting full RFC 1123 hostname validation — out of scope, would need to handle IPv6, IDN
punycode labels, underscore hostnames used on internal networks, etc.

### 3. Tests

For username/password trimming — trimming is in `load_config`, which we don't test directly
(requires env var coordination). No new test needed; it's a one-liner with clear intent.

For `smtp_server` bad-char checks — add two tests:

```rust
fn config_smtp_server_at_sign_errors()    // "user@smtp.example.com"  → err
fn config_smtp_server_url_scheme_errors() // "smtp://smtp.example.com" → err
```

## Verification

```bash
cargo fmt --check
cargo clippy -- -D warnings
cargo test
```

All 63 existing tests must continue to pass; 2 new tests added.

[package]
name = "ferrous-comics"
version = "1.1.2"  # overridden at release time by release.yml from the git tag
edition = "2024"
license = "MIT"

[dependencies]
anyhow     = "1.0"
chrono     = { version = "0.4", default-features = false, features = ["std"] }
dotenvy    = "0.15"
env_logger = { version = "0.11", default-features = false, features = ["auto-color"] }
lettre     = { version = "0.11", default-features = false, features = ["smtp-transport", "rustls-tls", "builder", "hostname"] }
log        = "0.4"
ureq       = { version = "2", default-features = false, features = ["tls", "json"] }
idna_adapter = "=1.0.0"                                    # stub out IDNA Unicode normalization (via
                                                           # url/idna used by both ureq and lettre);
                                                           # all hostnames must be ASCII/punycode —
                                                           # validated at startup for XKCD_SMTP_SERVER
serde      = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1.0"
redb       = "2"

[dev-dependencies]
tempfile = "3"
mockito  = "1"

[profile.release]
strip         = true    # remove debug symbols (~50-70% size reduction)
lto           = "thin"  # cross-crate dead-code elimination without fat-LTO compile cost
codegen-units = 1       # maximize intra-crate optimization
opt-level     = "z"     # optimize for size: 2.0 MiB vs 2.2 MiB ("s") vs 2.5 MiB ("3")
panic         = "abort" # saves ~300 KiB by stripping unwind tables from dependencies; this crate
                        # doesn't use catch_unwind, and for this CLI panics are treated as fatal
                        # either way — abort keeps the binary smaller and skips unwinding /
                        # running destructors on failure. redb uses a write-ahead log so an abort
                        # mid-run leaves the DB consistent; there are no connections to close
                        # gracefully (SMTP and HTTP are fire-and-forget per run)

input.old_string: env_logger = { version = "0.11", default-features = false, features = ["auto-color"] }
input.new_string: env_logger = { version = "0.11", default-features = false, features = ["auto-color", "humantime"] }

[dependencies]
anyhow     = "1.0"

env_logger = { version = "0.11", default-features = false, features = ["auto-color"] }
lettre     = { version = "0.11", default-features = false, features = ["smtp-transport", "rustls-tls", "builder", "hostname"] }
log        = "0.4"
ureq       = { version = "2", default-features = false, features = ["tls", "json"] }
idna_adapter = "=1.0.0"                                    # stub out IDNA Unicode normalization (via
                                                           # url/idna used by both ureq and lettre);
                                                           # all hostnames must be ASCII/punycode —
                                                           # validated at startup for XKCD_SMTP_SERVER
serde      = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1.0"
redb       = "2"

[dev-dependencies]
tempfile = "3"
mockito  = "1"

[profile.release]
strip         = true    # remove debug symbols (~50-70% size reduction)
lto           = "thin"  # cross-crate dead-code elimination without fat-LTO compile cost
codegen-units = 1       # maximize intra-crate optimization
opt-level     = "z"     # optimize for size: 2.0 MiB vs 2.2 MiB ("s") vs 2.5 MiB ("3")
panic         = "abort" # saves ~300 KiB by stripping unwind tables from dependencies; this crate
                        # doesn't use catch_unwind, and for this CLI panics are treated as fatal
                        # either way — abort keeps the binary smaller and skips unwinding /
                        # running destructors on failure. redb uses a write-ahead log so an abort

content.text:
Now I have a complete picture. Here is the full adversarial review:

---

## Adversarial Review: Adding `humantime` to env_logger features

### 1. Transitive dependencies — what's actually new

The Cargo.lock before this change had 161 packages. After, it has 165. The four new packages added are:

- `jiff 0.2.23`
- `jiff-static 0.2.23` (a proc-macro support crate for jiff)
- `portable-atomic` (pulled in by jiff, only for targets lacking atomic `ptr`)
- `portable-atomic-util` (same conditional target gate)

`serde_core` was already present (used by `serde` and `indexmap`).

**Naming gotcha worth noting:** In `env_logger` 0.11.x, the feature is still *called* `"humantime"` for backward compatibility, but it no longer uses the `humantime` crate. Instead `humantime = ["dep:jiff"]` — it pulls in `jiff` as the actual implementation. The `humantime` crate does not appear anywhere in the dependency graph. This is correct behavior; the feature name is a stable API surface even though the implementation changed upstream.

### 2. MSRV and security

- **MSRV**: `jiff 0.2.23` requires Rust 1.70. `env_logger 0.11.9` also requires 1.71. The project uses `stable` in CI with no pinned version, so this is not a problem in practice.
- **Security**: No local advisory database is present to query, but `jiff` is a pure-Rust, actively-maintained datetime library from BurntSushi with no known CVEs. `portable-atomic` is a well-audited crate used across the ecosystem. No concerns here.
- **Size impact**: `jiff`'s source is 3.6 MB, but `env_logger` only calls `jiff::Timestamp::try_from(SystemTime)` and formats it. With `opt-level = "z"`, `strip = true`, and thin LTO, the linker will dead-strip the vast majority of jiff. The actual binary size increase is expected to be small (rough estimate: tens of KB at most for a timestamp formatter). However, this is a real binary size regression from zero (no timestamp code at all) to some. Given the project explicitly tracks binary size and chose `opt-level = "z"`, this tradeoff should be acknowledged — though restoring timestamps is almost certainly worth the cost.

### 3. Can timestamps still be missing or broken after this change?

**No silent failure paths found.** Specifically:

- The `write_timestamp` function in `env_logger` is gated on `#[cfg(feature = "humantime")]`. Without the feature, the function is a no-op (returns `Ok(())` immediately). With the feature enabled, it calls `jiff::Timestamp::try_from(self.time)` — if that conversion fails (impossible in practice for a `SystemTime::now()` value within a valid range), it returns `fmt::Error`, which means the log line would be written *without* a timestamp rather than panicking. This is acceptable graceful degradation.
- `main.rs` uses `Builder::from_env(...).init()` with no custom format builder, so it uses `ConfigurableFormat::default()`, which sets `timestamp: Some(Default::default())` — i.e., second-precision timestamps are enabled by default. No code suppresses this.
- The timestamps are UTC RFC 3339 formatted (e.g., `2024-01-15T12:34:56Z`). This is correct and readable.

### 4. Anything else suspicious?

**One naming ambiguity to watch for:** The `Cargo.toml` feature name `"humantime"` refers to *jiff*, not the `humantime` crate. If someone in the future audits dependencies and searches for a crate literally named `humantime`, they won't find it — the feature name is a historical artifact. This is not a bug, but worth a comment if the project ever has a dependency audit process.

**No other issues.** The change is a minimal, correct fix: the `humantime` feature was dropped when `default-features = false` was added, the feature name `"humantime"` is the correct name to restore it in env_logger 0.11.x, and it resolves to jiff (not the old humantime crate) as the actual timestamp implementation.

### Summary

| Question | Finding |
|---|---|
| New transitive deps? | Yes: `jiff`, `jiff-static`, `portable-atomic`, `portable-atomic-util` (4 new crates) |
| MSRV/security issues? | None. jiff MSRV is 1.70; project uses stable. No known CVEs. |
| Timestamps still broken? | No. The default format enables timestamps; the feature gate is the only blocker. |
| Other concerns? | Minor: binary size increases slightly. The `"humantime"` feature name resolves to jiff (not the humantime crate) — not a bug, but a naming surprise. |

**Verdict: change is correct and safe to merge.**

gh pr create --title "Restore timestamps in log output" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

Log lines were missing timestamps:

```
[INFO  ferrous_comics] comic #3223 already seen, exiting
```

Root cause: `env_logger` was configured with `default-features = false` (added in #26 for binary size), which silently dropped the `humantime` feature. That feature gates the timestamp formatter — without it `env_logger`'s format code compiles to a no-op and every log line loses its timestamp.

Fix: add `"humantime"` to the env_logger feature list explicitly.

```
[2026-03-25T00:15:51Z INFO  ferrous_comics] comic #3223 already seen, exiting
```

Timestamps are UTC RFC 3339 at second precision, which is env_logger's default.

Note: in env_logger 0.11.x the `"humantime"` feature name is kept for API compatibility but the implementation now uses `jiff` rather than the old `humantime` crate. This adds 4 small transitive deps (`jiff`, `jiff-static`, `portable-atomic`, `portable-atomic-util`).

## Test plan

- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes
- [ ] `cargo test` — all 65 tests pass
- [ ] CI green
- [ ] Log output shows timestamps
EOF
)"

Note: in env_logger 0.11.x the `"humantime"` feature name is kept for API compatibility but the implementation now uses `jiff` rather than the old `humantime` crate. This adds 4 small transitive deps (`jiff`, `jiff-static`, `portable-atomic`, `portable-atomic-util`).

## Test plan

- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes
- [ ] `cargo test` — all 65 tests pass
- [ ] CI green
- [ ] Log output shows timestamps
EOF
)"

## Summary

Log lines were missing timestamps:

content.text:
PR: https://github.com/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics/pull/27

The cause: `default-features = false` on env_logger (added in #26) silently dropped `humantime`, which is the feature flag that gates the entire timestamp formatting path — without it the formatter compiles to a no-op. One word added to Cargo.toml fixes it.
